Subject: Tracking morning pulse for overtraining – please log this week

Ethan,

After talking today about you feeling a little more wiped than usual after back‑to‑back shifts and training, I’d like you to track your **morning resting pulse** for the next 7 days.

Here’s what you told me you’ve seen recently on your watch:
- Normal off‑day morning pulse: **around 59 bpm**
- The last three mornings: **67, 70, and 69 bpm**

That jump of ~10 beats is sometimes just stress, poor sleep, or dehydration, but it can be an early sign that you’re flirting with overtraining. Please set an alarm to measure your heart rate right after you wake up, before getting out of bed.

Log it in this format and text me a photo of the page Sunday night:

Date – Morning pulse – Notes (sleep, stress, soreness)
Example: 2/3 – 61 bpm – 6.5 hrs sleep, late homework, legs heavy from 400m repeats

If your morning pulse hits **75 bpm or higher** on any day, I want you to skip that day’s hard session and just do light mobility and a 10–15 minute easy jog.

Be honest with the numbers; this isn’t about toughness. Pushing through when your heart rate is elevated at rest is how small issues turn into bigger problems.

– Coach Ruiz

Subject: Follow-up on fueling and off‑season goals

Hi Ethan,

It was good to see you doing so well at yesterday’s check‑in.

As we discussed, your strength and performance markers look solid, and your current body composition is appropriate for a multi-sport high school athlete. I want to reiterate that our priority is consistency, not chasing a specific number on the scale. Your range over the past three months (between 158–162 lbs at the afternoon weigh-ins) is perfectly acceptable given fluctuations from training, hydration, and school stress.

Please continue the structured nutrition plan we built during your time in the Phoenix Children’s Adolescent Eating Disorder Program from August 2022 to March 2023. That plan was designed specifically to prevent the kind of disordered eating and excessive exercise behaviors that previously led to the 12‑pound weight drop and low-testosterone hormonal disruption in your junior-year soccer season.

Key reminders:
- No more than one weigh-in per week, preferably at the same time of day.
- Focus on performance cues: energy at practice, recovery, and mood.
- Keep the 3 meals + 2–3 snacks pattern, especially on double‑practice days.

Let’s tentatively plan another virtual follow-up in 6–8 weeks, unless you or your parents feel a need to check in sooner.

You’re doing the work—keep trusting the process.

Best,
Karen

Phoenix Children’s – Adolescent Medicine
